jsonLdWeb.name: Chain Link Fence Calculator
jsonLdWeb.description: Calculate chain link fence materials with a complete itemized list of all components.

mainEntity.name: How much chain link mesh do I need?

acceptedAnswer.text: Chain link fabric is sold in 50-foot rolls. Divide your fence length by 50 and round up to get the number of rolls. For a 150-foot fence, you need 3 rolls. Buy one extra roll for a long fence to account for waste around corners and gates.

mainEntity.name: How far apart are chain link fence posts?

acceptedAnswer.text: Line posts are spaced every 10 feet on center. Terminal posts (end, corner, and gate posts) are placed at every corner, end of run, and on each side of a gate opening. Terminal posts are larger and set deeper than line posts.

mainEntity.name: What are terminal posts vs line posts?

acceptedAnswer.text: Terminal posts are the larger, heavier posts at corners, ends, and gates — typically 2-7/8" diameter for residential fences. Line posts are the smaller intermediate posts every 10 feet — typically 1-5/8" diameter. Terminal posts anchor the tension wire and fabric.

mainEntity.name: How much top rail do I need?

acceptedAnswer.text: Top rail comes in 10.5 ft or 21 ft lengths that sleeve together. Divide fence length by 10.5 and round up. For 150 feet: 15 pieces of 10.5 ft rail. Top rail runs continuously from terminal post to terminal post.

mainEntity.name: How many fence ties do I need?

acceptedAnswer.text: Use 1 tie per foot of top rail and 3 ties per line post (top, middle, bottom). For a 150 ft fence with 14 line posts: 150 rail ties + 42 post ties = 192 ties. Buy ties in bags of 100.

mainEntity.name: Do I need tension wire for chain link?

acceptedAnswer.text: Tension wire (bottom wire) is recommended but not always required. It runs along the bottom of the fence to prevent animals from pushing the mesh up. It also keeps the bottom edge straight. Plan for one run the full fence length plus 10%.

chartHTML:
Chain Link Fence Components Reference

Standard component specifications for residential chain link fences.

ComponentSpecificationSpacing / Quantity Rule
Chain Link Mesh50 ft rolls, 2" diamond1 roll per 50 ft of fence
Line Posts1-5/8" dia, galvanizedEvery 10 ft on center
Terminal Posts2-7/8" dia, galvanizedEach corner, end, gate side
Top Rail1-3/8" dia, 10.5 ft lengths1 per 10.5 ft of fence
Fence TiesAluminum wire ties1/ft rail + 3/line post
Tension Wire7 or 9 gauge galv.Full fence length + 10%
howItWorks:
How to Calculate Chain Link Fence Materials

Chain link fences have several components: mesh fabric, line posts, terminal posts, top rail, ties, caps, and tension hardware.

Line Posts = (Fence Length ÷ 10) − 1 − Terminal Posts in line
  • Mesh rolls: fence length ÷ 50, rounded up
  • Terminal posts: corners + ends + (gates × 2)
  • Line posts: every 10 ft between terminal posts
  • Top rail: fence length ÷ 10.5, rounded up
  • Ties: 1 per ft of rail + 3 per line post
  • Tension bars: 1 per terminal post
  • Tension bands: 1 per ft of fence height per terminal post
faqHTML:
Frequently Asked Questions

How much chain link mesh do I need?

Mesh comes in 50 ft rolls. Divide fence length by 50 and round up. For 150 ft: 3 rolls. Add an extra partial roll for corners and gates.

How far apart are chain link posts?

Line posts every 10 ft. Terminal posts (larger) at every corner, end of run, and each side of gates.

Terminal posts vs line posts?

Terminal posts are 2-7/8" diameter at corners, ends, and gates. Line posts are 1-5/8" diameter at 10 ft intervals between terminal posts.

How much top rail?

Top rail comes in 10.5 ft sleevable lengths. Divide fence length by 10.5 and round up.

How many fence ties?

1 tie per foot of rail plus 3 ties per line post. Buy in bags of 100.

Do I need tension wire?

Recommended along the bottom to prevent mesh from being pushed up. Plan for full fence length plus 10%.

sections.title: What Is the Chain Link Fence Calculator?
sections.body: The Chain Link Fence Calculator is a free online tool designed for contractors and DIY builders who need quick, accurate calculations in the construction and building space. By entering your total fence length, fence height, corner posts, you get instant results including mesh rolls, line posts, terminal posts. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting mesh rolls right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In construction and building, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by contractors and DIY builders worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to plan your projects with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your total fence length and need to find the right mesh rolls. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Total Fence Length (ft)
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your total fence length — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: fence height, corner posts, end posts, gates. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your mesh rolls, line posts, terminal posts. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your total fence length before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.

Pay attention to both mesh rolls and line posts — they work together to give you the full picture.

faq.question: Is the Chain Link Fence Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard construction and building formulas trusted by contractors and DIY builders. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

faq.question: What total fence length should I enter?
faq.answer: Enter the most accurate total fence length value you have available. If you're estimating, use a conservative figure. You can always run the calculator again with different values to see how changes affect the results.

faq.question: Can I use this calculator on my phone?
faq.answer: Yes, the Chain Link Fence Calculator is fully responsive and works on any device — phone, tablet, or desktop. No app download needed.

faq.question: What does mesh rolls mean in my results?
faq.answer: The mesh rolls value represents the primary output of the calculation based on your inputs. Use it as a baseline figure for your construction and building planning and decision-making.
